---
title: "Statistical Analysis Plan: [title]"
subtitle: 'DOCUMENT: SAP-yyyy-NNN-XX-v01'
author: '**From:** Felipe Figueiredo **To:** ___'
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  html_document:
    fig_caption: yes
    fig_height: 6
    fig_width: 6
    keep_md: yes
    number_sections: yes
    toc: yes
  pdf_document:
    number_sections: yes
    toc: yes
  word_document:
    fig_caption: yes
    fig_height: 6
    fig_width: 6
    keep_md: yes
    reference_docx: misc/style_SAP_en.docx
    toc: yes
toc-title: "Table of Contents"
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
# ************************************************************
# Template: SAP_OBS_full_en.Rmd
# Version:  0.xx.x
# Author:   Felipe Figueiredo
# Created:  2026-03-22
#
# ************************************************************
# QC Date:  YYYY-MM-DD
# ************************************************************

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE)
options(scipen = 999)
library(pander)
library(knitr)
library(flextable)
library(officer)
library(here)

knitr::opts_knit$set(root.dir = here())
panderOptions('table.style', 'rmarkdown')
panderOptions('table.split.table', 160)

# Initialize counters for Table indices
ind.table.body <- 0
ind.table.app <- 0
```

---

\newpage

**Statistical Analysis Plan: [title]**

**Document version**

```{r history}
# Define each version as an atomic row
v1 <- c("01", "YYYY-MM-DD", "Initial version.")

# Bind rows together
# Using naming in rbind automatically sets the row labels if needed, 
# but here we just want a clean table.
# Convert to data frame and set names
history <- tibble::as_tibble(rbind(v1), .name_repair = "minimal")
colnames(history) <- c("Version", "Date", "Alterations")

# Render
knitr::kable(
  history,
  align = "ccl",
)
```

```{r cleaning, include=FALSE}
# Load cached results (Data prep, data mockup, sample size calculation, etc.)
# This ensures all analysis outputs are available when compiling the report.
# Source 96- will safely check for and load all objects.
source(here("02 scripts", "96_load_cached_results.R"), encoding = 'UTF-8')
```

---

# Abbreviations

- AIC: Akaike Information Criterion
- AUC: Area Under the Curve
- BMI: Body Mass Index
- CCS: Complete Case Set
- CI: Confidence Interval
- CV: Coefficient of Variation
- d: Cohen's d Effect Size
- DAG: Directed Acyclic Graph
- EDC: Electronic Data Capture
- FAS: Full Analysis Set
- GLM: Generalized Linear Model
- h: Cohen's h Effect Size
- HR: Hazard Ratio
- IAS: Imputed Analysis Set
- ICD: International Classification of Diseases
- IPTW: Inverse Probability of Treatment Weighting
- IQR: Interquartile Range
- IRR: Incidence Rate Ratio
- LMM: Linear Mixed Model
- LogMM: Logistic Mixed Model
- MCAR: Missing Completely at Random
- MAR: Missing at Random
- MICE: Multiple Imputation by Chained Equations
- MNAR: Missing Not at Random
- OR: Odds Ratio
- ROC: Receiver Operating Curve
- RR: Risk Ratio
- SAP: Statistical Analysis Plan
- SAR: Statistical Analytic Report
- SAS: Sensitivity Analysis Set
- SD: Standard Deviation
- SE: Standard Error
- STROBE: Strengthening the Reporting of Observational Studies in Epidemiology
- VIF: Variance Inflation Factor

# Introduction

## Context and Rationale

[Analyst Note: Briefly describe the clinical rationale and the motivation behind the research. This section provides the "Why" that guides the "How."]

## Research Questions and Objectives

[Analyst Note: Explicitly state the Primary, Secondary, and Exploratory objectives using the list format below. Each objective should be framed as a testable statistical hypothesis (e.g., "To compare the odds of [Outcome] between [Group A] and [Group B]").]

- **Primary:** To compare the [odds/risk] of [Outcome] in [Group B] participants, when compared to [Group A].
- **Secondary:**
    1. Objective S1
    2. Objective S2
    3. Objective S3
- **Exploratory:**
    1. Objective E1
    2. Objective E2
    3. Objective E3

# Study Design and Population

## Study Design

[Analyst Note: Define the study architecture (e.g., Cross-sectional, Retrospective Cohort, Case-Control). Explicitly state the observational framework and the direction of data collection. Reference compliance with relevant guidelines (e.g., STROBE, RECORD, ...). Define the Primary Effect Measure here.]

**Recommended reporting guideline**

The EQUATOR network reporting guidelines (<http://www.equator-network.org/>) have seen increasing adoption by scientific journals.
All observational studies are recommended to be reported following the STROBE guideline (von Elm et al, 2014).
<!-- In particular when a retrospective study is conducted using hospital records, it is recommended that the RECORD extension of the STROBE guideline is considered (Benchimol et al, 2015). -->
<!-- All clinical trials are recommended to be reported following the CONSORT guideline (Schulz K F, Altman D G, Moher D., 2010). -->
<!-- All systematic reviews are recommended to be reported following the PRISMA guideline (Page et al, 2021). -->
<!-- All protocols for systematic reviews are recommended to be reported following the PRISMA-P guideline (Page et al, 2021). -->

## Study Population Definitions

[Analyst Note: Describe the "person" of the study. Distinguish between the Target Population (who the results apply to), the Source Population (the database/registry), and the Analytic Unit (e.g., "The individual patient," "The hospital admission," "The surgical procedure").]

## Setting and Time Period

[Analyst Note: Describe the "place and time" of the study. Define the physical or virtual location of the study (e.g., "Multicenter database," "Single-unit surgical clinic"). Define the period covered in the study: start/end dates of data collection and the follow-up duration if applicable. Include Index date and Censoring if applicable.]

## Inclusion and Exclusion Criteria

[Analyst Note: Use a bulleted list to define the "Theoretical Bouncer." These are clinical and demographic filters (e.g., "Age > 18," "Confirmed diagnosis of X"). This section defines the ideal cohort before accounting for data missingness.]

**Inclusion Criteria**

Participants will be included if they fulfill all of these criteria:

- crit 1
- crit 2

**Exclusion Criteria**

Participants will be excluded if they fulfill any of these criteria:

- exc 1
- exc 2

# Variables

## Exposures

[Analyst Note: Define the independent variables. Specify the Primary Exposure and any secondary exposures. Note the scale of measurement (e.g., Binary, Continuous, Multilevel Categorical).]

## Outcomes

[Analyst Note: Define the dependent variables. Use the Zarin (2011) framework below to ensure each outcome is precise enough to be programmed without ambiguity.]

```{r outcomes}
# --- Define Levels for clarity ---
# Level 1: Domain
# Level 2: Specific Measurement
# Level 3: Specific Metric (End value, Change from baseline, Time-to-event)
# Level 4: Method of Aggregation (Mean, Proportion, Odds, Hazard, Incidence Rate)

# --- Internal Registry (Scaffold) ---
# Format: c(Domain, Measurement, Metric, Method of Aggregation)

# Examples
Ex.RR <- c("Example RR", "Mortality", "Hospital Discharge Status", "End value", "Proportion")
Ex.OR <- c("Example OR", "Mortality", "ICD-10 Code", "End value", "Odds")
Ex.HR <- c("Example HR", "Mortality", "ICD-10 Code", "Time-to-event", "Hazard")
Ex.IR <- c("Example IR", "Mortality", "ICD-10 + Duration", "End value", "Incidence Rate")

# Project Outcomes (Fill these in)
P1    <- c("Outcome P1", "Domain", "Measurement", "End value", "Method of Aggregation")
S1    <- c("Outcome S1", "Domain", "Measurement", "End value", "Method of Aggregation")
S2    <- c("Outcome S2", "Domain", "Measurement", "End value", "Method of Aggregation")
S3    <- c("Outcome S3", "Domain", "Measurement", "End value", "Method of Aggregation")
E1    <- c("Outcome E1", "Domain", "Measurement", "End value", "Method of Aggregation")

# --- Combine and Format ---
# Using as.data.frame + t() to ensure row-wise binding with labels
outcomes <- rbind(
  Ex.RR,
  Ex.OR,
  Ex.HR,
  Ex.IR,
  P1,
  S1,
  S2,
  S3,
  E1
) %>% as_tibble(.name_repair = "minimal")

colnames(outcomes) <- c("Outcome", "Domain (L1)", "Measurement (L2)", "Metric (L3)", "Method of Aggregation (L4)")

# --- Render Table ---
# Toggle this block to 'eval: false' or comment out for client delivery
knitr::kable(
  outcomes,
  caption = "Zarin (2011) Outcome Specification Scaffold (Internal Use Only)",
  align = "cllll",
)
```

**Primary outcome**

**Secondary outcomes**

- "Outcome S1"
- "Outcome S2"
- "Outcome S3

**Exploratory outcomes**

- "Outcome E1"

## Other Variables and Covariates

[Analyst Note: List potential confounders. Distinguish between "Known Confounders" (based on literature/DAGs) and "Candidate Variables" for automated selection if applicable. Criteria for selection should be described in the Statistical Methods section. Describe derived variables.]

## Data Management

[Analyst Note: Dataset(s)/Database(s) name(s) and origin, linkage methods, Recoding, class coercions, standardized naming, as applicable.]

Data will be sourced from [X], cleaned via [standardized naming/coercion], and validated for range/logic consistency.

# Statistical Analyses Methods

## Analysis Sets

- Full Analysis Set (FAS)

[Analyst Note: e.g., all records meeting selection criteria]

- Complete Case Set (CCS)

[Analyst Note: Records with non-missing values for specific variables.]

- Imputed Analysis Set (IAS)

[Analyst Note: Optional, as needed per project.]

- Sensitivity Analysis Set (SAS)

[Analyst Note: Optional, as needed per project.]

## Study Size and Power

[Analyst Note: Justification of available N or power check]

## General Principles

[Analyst Note: Descriptive methods, digit precision, Complex Survey Design logic, time-to-event methods, and reporting of missingness]

The participant
<!-- baseline -->
characteristics will be described overall and by
exposure.
Demographic
(sex and age)
and clinical variables
<!-- ( [vars] ) -->
will be described as
mean (SD)
<!-- median (IQR) -->
or as counts and proportions (%), as appropriate.
The distributions of participants' characteristics will be summarized in tables and visualized in exploratory plots.
Missing data will be reported in tables as counts and proportions (%).

## Missing Data Handling

[Analyst Note: Follow a tiered approach (Van Buuren, 2018). <5%: CCA. 5-20%: MI. 20-40%: MI + Sensitivity. >40%: Drop.]

No missing data imputation will be performed.
All evaluations will be performed as complete case analyses.

<!-- Variables with missingness exceeding 40% will be excluded from multivariable adjustment due to high informational noise and potential model instability. -->
<!-- If all remaining variables have at most 5% missing data, no missing data imputation will be performed and all evaluations will be performed as complete case analyses (Van Buuren, 2018). -->
<!-- If there are variables with 5% to 40% missingness, Multiple Imputation by Chained Equations (MICE) with 40 iterations will be employed to preserve statistical power and minimize selection bias (White et al., 2011). -->
<!-- The mechanism of missingness will be assumed to be Missing At Random (MAR). -->

<!-- A sensitivity analysis comparing CCA and MI results will be mandatory only if there are variables where missingness exceeds 20%, or for the primary outcome regardless of the missingness level. -->

## Analysis of Primary Objective

[Analyst Note: ]

## Analysis of Secondary and Exploratory Objectives

[Analyst Note: If not applicable, set to N/A.]

- Analysis of Secondary Objective 1

[Analyst Note: Remove subsection if not scoped]

- Analysis of Secondary Objective 2

[Analyst Note: Remove subsection if not scoped]

- Analysis of Secondary Objective 3

[Analyst Note: Remove subsection if not scoped]

- Analysis of Exploratory Objective 1

[Analyst Note: Remove subsection if not scoped]

<!-- All comparisons between groups will be performed as univariate analyses. -->
<!-- Continuous variables will be compared between groups with the -->
<!-- independent t test with Welch correction. -->
<!-- paired t test. -->
<!-- Mann-Whitney test. -->
<!-- signed rank Wilcoxon test. -->
<!-- Differences in distribution of categorical variables will be assessed with the -->
<!-- Fisher exact test. -->
<!-- chi-square test. -->
<!-- McNemar test. -->

## Significance and Confidence Intervals

[Analyst Note: Alpha levels, tails]

All analyses will be performed using the significance level of 5%.
All significance hypothesis tests and confidence intervals computed will be
two-tailed.
<!-- left-tailed. -->
<!-- right-tailed. -->

## Statistical Software

[Analyst Note: R version, package management]

This analysis will be performed using the statistical software `R` (currently on version `r getRversion()`).

# Limitations and Observations

[Analyst Note: Model constraints, sample size issues, possible assumption violations and data source-specific limitations]

# References

- **SAR-yyyy-NNN-XX-v01** -- [title]
- Zarin DA, et al. (2011). *The ClinicalTrials.gov results database -- update and key issues.* NEJM. (<https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMsa1012065>).
- Gamble, C., et al. (2017). *Guidelines for the Content of Statistical Analysis Plans in Clinical Trials.* JAMA. (<https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.2017.18556>)
- von Elm, E., et al. (2014). *The Strengthening the Reporting of Observational Studies in Epidemiology (STROBE) Statement: guidelines for reporting observational studies.* Int J Surg. (<https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijsu.2014.07.013>)
- Benchimol, E. I., et al. (2015). *The REporting of studies Conducted using Observational Routinely-collected health Data (RECORD) statement.* PLoS Med. (<https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pmed.1001885>).
- Schulz, K. F., Altman, D. G., & Moher, D. (2010). *CONSORT 2010 Statement: updated guidelines for reporting parallel group randomised trials.* BMJ. (<https://doi.org/10.1136/bmj.c332>).
- Page, M. J., et al. (2021). *The PRISMA 2020 statement: an updated guideline for reporting systematic reviews.* BMJ. (<https://doi.org/10.1136/bmj.n71>).
- Moher, D., et al. (2015). *Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis Protocols (PRISMA-P) 2015 statement.* Syst Rev. (<https://doi.org/10.1186/2046-4053-4-1>).
- Cohen, J. (1988). *Statistical Power Analysis for the Behavioral Sciences (2nd Ed.).* Routledge.
- Harrell, F. E. (2015). *Regression Modeling Strategies: With Applications to Linear Models, Logistic and Ordinal Regression, and Survival Analysis (2nd Ed.).* Springer.
- Fox, J., & Weisberg, S. (2019). *An R Companion to Applied Regression (3rd Ed.).* SAGE Publications.
- Hosmer, D. W., Lemeshow, S., & Sturdivant, R. X. (2013). *Applied Logistic Regression (3rd Ed.).* Wiley.
- Fitzmaurice, G. M., Laird, N. M., & Ware, J. H. (2011). *Applied Longitudinal Analysis (2nd Ed.).* Wiley.
- van Buuren, S. (2018). *Flexible Imputation of Missing Data (2nd Ed.).* CRC Press.
- White IR, Royston P, Wood AM., (2011). *Multiple imputation using chained equations: Issues and guidance for practice.* Stat Med. (<https://doi.org/10.1002/sim.4067>)
- Lumley, T. (2010). *Complex Surveys: A Guide to Analysis Using R.* Wiley.
- Venables, W. N., & Ripley, B. D. (2002). *Modern Applied Statistics with S (4th Ed.).* Springer.
- Rothman, K. J. (2012). *Epidemiology: An Introduction (2nd Ed.).* Oxford University Press.
- Lash, T. L., VanderWeele, T. J., Haneuse, S., & Rothman, K. J. (2020). *Modern Epidemiology (4th Ed.).* Lippincott Williams & Wilkins.

# Appendix

This document was elaborated following recommendations on the structure for Statistical Analysis Plans (Gamble, 2017) for better transparency and clarity.

## Associated analyses

This analysis is part of a larger project and is supported by other analyses, linked below.

**[assoc_title]**

<[assoc_link]>

<!-- **[assoc_title]** -->

<!-- <[assoc_link]> -->

## Availability

All documents from this consultation were included in the consultant's Portfolio.

<!-- The client has requested that this analysis be kept confidential until a future date, determined by the client. -->
<!-- All documents from this consultation are therefore not published online and only the title and year of the analysis will be included in the consultant's Portfolio. -->
<!-- After the agreed date is reached, the documents will be released. -->

<!-- The client has requested that this analysis be kept confidential. -->
<!-- All documents from this consultation are therefore not published online and only the title and year of the analysis will be included in the consultant's Portfolio. -->

The portfolio is available at:

<https://philsf-biostat.github.io/SAR-yyyy-NNN-XX/>
